frameworks.name: limited resource hypothesis
frameworks.one_line: movement planning draws on a shared, limited pool of precision-determining resources

frameworks.name: working memory resource models
frameworks.one_line: analogy that motor plans share resources like items in working memory

core_question: Do concurrently prepared movement plans share a limited resource, such that planning more reduces the precision of each?
key_assumptions: movement plans, like working memory representations, may draw on a shared limited resource
initial movement direction variability indexes the precision of the underlying plan
delayed response cueing isolates the number of plans prepared before movement onset
manipulations: number of planned movements: one vs two (across targets or hands)
cue specifying target vs cue specifying hand
whether trajectory could be fully specified in advance
key_findings: initial movement direction variability was higher in two-plan than one-plan conditions across targets
the same cost appeared when two hands rather than two targets were planned
the one-plan precision advantage emerged only when the trajectory could be fully specified
interpretations: planning multiple movements incurs a precision cost consistent with a shared, limited planning resource
the cost reflects specification of full motor plans rather than general motor preparedness
